1:javascript:1: The solution first sorts the array numerically using nums sort with a comparison function that subtracts b from a, which is necessary because JavaScript’s default sort method converts elements to strings and would otherwise order them incorrectly; sorting groups elements so an optimal frequency window is always contiguous.
1:javascript:2: A for loop moves right across the array, adding the value at nums right to a running total that represents the sum of the current window’s elements.
1:javascript:3: The condition that nums at right times the size of the window, meaning right minus left plus one, minus total checks whether increasing every element in the window up to the value at nums at right would need more than k increments; if it does, the while loop moves left forward and subtracts nums at left from total until the window becomes affordable again.
1:javascript:4: The best value is updated after every window adjustment by comparing it with the current window size, and the largest valid window size found so far is returned as the maximum achievable frequency.
1:javascript:5: This two-pointer technique keeps the scanning itself linear, so the overall complexity is big O of n log n because of the initial sort.
1:python:1: The algorithm begins by calling nums dot sort, which is essential: once the array is sorted, any optimal group of equal elements can be represented as a contiguous window, since it is always cheaper to raise smaller elements up to a nearby larger one than to bridge a gap across values that are not in order.
1:python:2: The right pointer expands the window one element at a time in the for loop, adding the value of nums at the right position to the running total variable, which represents the sum of all elements currently inside the window.
1:python:3: The key inequality nums at right times one plus right minus left minus total computes exactly how many increments would be needed to raise every element in the window up to the value at nums at right; when this value exceeds k, the while loop advances left and subtracts the value at nums at left from total, shrinking the window until the operation budget is respected again.
1:python:4: After each adjustment, set best to the larger of its current value and right minus left plus one, which gives the largest feasible window size and corresponds to the maximum achievable frequency.
1:python:5: Because the window only ever expands or contracts monotonically, the total work is linear in n after the sort that takes linearithmic time, making this an efficient two pointer sliding window solution.
1:java:1: The algorithm begins by sorting the nums array, which groups elements with equal cost next to each other, so that any optimal answer can be represented as a contiguous window over the sorted array.
1:java:2: A for loop moves rightward, adding the value of nums at right into a running long total that tracks the sum of the current window; total is declared as a long specifically to avoid overflow once it accumulates values from up to ten to the fifth elements.
1:java:3: The core check, namely that long times nums at right multiplied by right minus left plus one, minus total is greater than k, makes the multiplication happen in long before comparing against k, because nums at right times right minus left plus one could otherwise overflow a thirty two bit integer; whenever this cost is greater than k, the inner while loop advances left and subtracts nums at left from total to shrink the window.
1:java:4: Each iteration updates the variable best to be the larger of its current value and the length of the current window, calculated as right minus left plus one, so it tracks the biggest window that remains within the operation budget, and this tracked value is returned as the final answer.
1:java:5: The overall approach runs in time proportional to n times the logarithm of n, dominated by the sort, while the two pointer scan itself runs in linear time.
1:cpp:1: The algorithm sorts the vector in place, using the sort function over the full range from the beginning to the end, which is what allows any optimal group of equal elements to be treated as a contiguous window rather than scattered indices.
1:cpp:2: A for loop advances to the right while maintaining a running total of type long long, which tracks the sum of the elements currently in the window; the total is declared as long long specifically to prevent overflow, since the array can contain up to one hundred thousand elements, each as large as one hundred thousand.
1:cpp:3: The condition that casts nums at the right index to long long, then multiplies it by one more than the difference between the right and left indices, and subtracts total, before comparing the result to k, explicitly forces the multiplication to use long long so it can exceed what a 32 bit int can hold; when the cost becomes too high, the while loop moves left forward and subtracts nums at the left index from total to shrink the window back within the allowed budget.
1:cpp:4: The best value is updated every time a valid window is found, so it always tracks the maximum frequency you can achieve, and it is returned after the loop has scanned the entire array.
1:cpp:5: Overall the solution runs in order of n times log n time, dominated by the sort, with the sliding window itself contributing only order n time.
2:javascript:1: The parse closure normalizes the expression by splitting it on the minus sign and joining the parts with plus and a negative sign, turning every subtraction into an explicit addition of a negative term so the string can be split uniformly on the plus sign.
2:javascript:2: For each term, if it includes an x, the code treats everything before the x as the sign: if it is an empty string, it means an implicit coefficient of positive one, if it is a hyphen, it means an implicit coefficient of negative one, and otherwise it parses the value as an explicit integer using parseInt.
2:javascript:3: Terms without an x are instead added to a running constant total, so each side of the equation produces a coefficient and constant pair.
2:javascript:4: The two sides are combined by subtracting the right side’s values from the left side’s values, collapsing the equation into a single coefficient times x equals a constant form.
2:javascript:5: If the coefficient is zero, the function returns “Infinite solutions” when the constant is also zero, or returns “No solution” otherwise, and if the coefficient is not zero, it returns a template string that gives x equal to the constant divided by the coefficient as the final answer.
2:python:1: The parse helper normalizes the expression by replacing every minus sign with plus and minus, which lets the entire string be split uniformly on plus into separate signed terms.
2:python:2: For each term, if it contains the letter x, the code examines everything before the x character as the sign; when the sign is empty it means an implicit coefficient of plus one, when the sign is minus it means an implicit coefficient of minus one, and otherwise the sign is parsed as an explicit integer coefficient.
2:python:3: Terms that do not contain an x are accumulated into a constant term instead, so that each side of the equation gets a coefficient and constant pair.
2:python:4: The two sides are then combined by subtracting the right side’s coefficient and constant from the left side’s, producing a single coefficient and constant that represent coefficient times x equals constant.
2:python:5: Finally, if the coefficient is zero, the code returns “Infinite solutions” or “No solution,” depending on whether the constant is also zero, and otherwise it returns x equals the constant divided by the coefficient as the unique integer solution.
2:java:1: The parse helper first calls expression replace of a minus sign with plus sign followed by a negative sign, so that every subtraction becomes an explicit addition of a negative term, allowing the entire expression to be split uniformly on plus.
2:java:2: For each resulting term, if it contains the letter x, the substring before x is treated as the sign: an empty sign means an implicit positive one coefficient, a minus sign means an implicit negative one coefficient, and any other value is parsed as an explicit integer coefficient using Integer parse int.
2:java:3: Terms that do not contain the letter x are added to a running constant total instead, so each side of the equation is represented by a coefficient and a constant pair.
2:java:4: The two pairs on the sides are then combined by subtracting the right side’s coefficient and constant from the left side’s, resulting in a single equation where the coefficient times x equals the constant.
2:java:5: If the coefficient equals zero, the method returns “infinite solutions” or “no solution” depending on whether the constant is also zero, and otherwise it returns “x equals” followed by the integer division of the constant by the coefficient.
2:cpp:1: The private parse helper builds a normalized string by copying the expression one character at a time and replacing every minus sign with plus dash, so the entire expression can later be tokenized uniformly on plus signs.
2:cpp:2: A string stream is used with getline from the stream to read each signed term one at a time from the normalized string, splitting on plus signs.
2:cpp:3: For each term that contains an x, the part of the substring before the x is treated as the sign: if that part is empty, it means an implicit plus one coefficient, if it is a minus sign, it means an implicit minus one coefficient, and otherwise that part is parsed as an explicit numeric coefficient using stoi, while terms that do not contain an x are added to a running constant.
2:cpp:4: Each side of the equation produces a pair of integers, consisting of a coefficient and a constant, and the two sides are combined by subtracting the right side’s pair from the left side’s pair to isolate a single coefficient times x equals a constant relationship.
2:cpp:5: If the coefficient is zero, the method returns “Infinite solutions” or “No solution,” depending on whether the constant term is also zero; otherwise, it returns “x equals” followed by the integer-divided result of the constant term divided by the coefficient.
3:javascript:1: The solution adds a trailing plus sign to the input string so the last number is processed using the same logic as every other term, avoiding the need for a separate step after the loop.
3:javascript:2: Digits are detected using a character range check, meaning the character is at least the letter zero and at most the letter nine, and they are accumulated into num by multiplying num by ten and then adding the numeric value of the character, so multi digit values are built.
3:javascript:3: When a character that is neither a digit nor a space appears, the previous sign determines the operation: plus pushes the current number, minus pushes the negative of the current number, multiply pops the stack backed by an array and pushes the product, and divide pops the top value and pushes the result of truncating toward zero, using Math trunc of the previous value divided by the current number.
3:javascript:4: After the operator has been handled, the sign is updated and the number is reset to zero so the next number can begin accumulating.
3:javascript:5: Because multiplication and division are applied immediately to the stack while addition and subtraction are delayed, the final stack reduction that starts with zero and adds each remaining value correctly totals the expression in a single linear pass.
3:python:1: The solution appends a trailing plus sign to the input string so the last number in the expression is guaranteed to be processed through the same logic as every other term, avoiding a special case after the loop.
3:python:2: As it scans each character, it accumulates digit characters into num by setting num to num multiplied by ten plus the integer value of the character, building up multi digit numbers one character at a time.
3:python:3: When a character that is neither a digit nor a space is encountered, the sign variable from the previously seen number determines what to do with the completed num: a plus sign adds num to the stack, a minus sign adds negative num to the stack, a multiplication sign removes the top of the stack and adds the product back, and a division sign removes the top of the stack and adds the integer result of the previous value divided by num, truncated toward zero.
3:python:4: After applying the operation, the sign is updated to the new operator, and the number is reset to zero so the next number can be accumulated.
3:python:5: Because multiplication and division are carried out right away using the stack, while addition and subtraction are postponed as signed pushes, the final sum of the stack correctly follows operator precedence in a single pass that runs in linear time.
3:java:1: The algorithm adds a trailing plus sign to the string so the final number is always processed through the same code path as every other term, eliminating the need for any special-case handling after the loop.
3:java:2: A Deque of integers is used like a stack, and digit characters are gathered into a number called num by multiplying num by ten and then adding the value of the character minus the character zero, using Character dot is digit to tell whether a character is a digit.
3:java:3: When an operator is reached, the previously stored sign value determines how to handle the completed number: plus pushes num, minus pushes negative num, times pops the stack and pushes the product of the popped value and num, and divide pops the stack and pushes the integer-divided result.
3:java:4: The sign is then updated to the current character, and the number is reset to zero before continuing the scan.
3:java:5: Since multiplication and division are worked out immediately using the stack, while addition and subtraction are postponed as signed values, adding up the stack at the end gives the correct result in linear time and linear space.
3:cpp:1: The algorithm adds a trailing plus sign to the string so the last number is guaranteed to be handled through the same code path as every other term, avoiding any special-case logic after the loop.
3:cpp:2: A vector of integers is used as a stack by using push back and back, and digits are collected into num by checking with is digit and then updating num to be num times ten plus the numeric value of the character ch minus the character zero.
3:cpp:3: When an operator is reached, the previously stored sign determines the action: plus pushes the current number onto the stack, minus pushes the negative of the current number onto the stack, multiply updates the top value of the stack by multiplying it with the current number in place, and divide updates the top value of the stack by dividing it by the current number in place, using C plus plus built in integer division that truncates toward zero.
3:cpp:4: The sign is then updated to match the current character, and num is reset to zero so it can accumulate the next number.
3:cpp:5: Because multiplication and division immediately modify the top of the stack, while addition and subtraction are postponed as signed value pushes, adding up the stack only at the end produces the correct result in linear time.
4:javascript:1: The function first sorts nums numerically using nums sort with a minus b, grouping values so that a balanced subset, where the maximum is less than or equal to the minimum times k, can be represented as a contiguous window in the sorted order.
4:javascript:2: A for loop moves from left to right across the array, and for each right position, an inner while loop moves the left position further left past any element where the value at nums right is greater than the value at nums left times k, keeping the window’s boundaries within the allowed ratio.
4:javascript:3: After adjusting the left pointer, best is updated to the larger of the current best value or the difference between right and left minus one, so it records the largest window size observed using this offset between the two pointers.
4:javascript:4: Because the left pointer only moves forward during the entire scan, the two-pointer pass takes linear time, and the overall runtime is dominated by the sorting step, which takes time proportional to n times the logarithm of n.
4:javascript:5: The function returns n minus the best value, converting the size of the largest window that can be kept into the smallest number of elements that must be removed.
4:python:1: The algorithm begins by sorting nums, which groups values so that any balanced subset, where the maximum is less than or equal to the minimum times k, can be represented as a contiguous window in the sorted order.
4:python:2: An outer for loop moves from left to right across the array, and for each position labeled right, an inner while loop moves the left pointer further left past any element where the value at right is greater than the value at left times k, ensuring that the window made of left and right always satisfies the balance condition relative to its current bounds.
4:python:3: After adjusting left, best equals the maximum of best and right minus left minus one, recording the size of the largest balanced window found so far using this particular offset.
4:python:4: Because both pointers only move forward and never backtrack, the overall scan after sorting takes linear time, so the total time complexity is linear time multiplied by a logarithm of n, dominated by the sort.
4:python:5: The final answer, n minus best, converts the largest kept window size into the minimum number of elements that must be removed from the original array.
4:java:1: The method starts by calling array sort on the input, so that any balanced subset of the array—meaning its maximum value is no more than the minimum value multiplied by k—can be represented as a contiguous window over the sorted values.
4:java:2: A for loop moves from left to right through the array, and at each position it runs a nested while loop that moves left past any element where the value at nums[right] is greater than the value at nums[left] times k, using a long cast on the multiplication to prevent 32-bit overflow when both k and nums[left] are large.
4:java:3: After the window is adjusted, best is updated to the larger of itself or the value of right minus left minus one, which tracks the largest valid window size seen for this particular offset between the pointers.
4:java:4: Because the left pointer only ever moves forward across the entire scan, the two-pointer portion takes linear time after the sorting step, which takes order n log n time.
4:java:5: The method returns n minus the best value, converting the size of the largest window that can be kept into the smallest number of elements that must be removed.
4:cpp:1: The function begins by sorting the vector, using sort of the numbers from beginning to end, which groups the values so that a balanced subset, where the maximum value is at most the minimum value times k, can be represented as a contiguous window over the sorted array.
4:cpp:2: A for loop moves right across the vector, and for each position, an inner while loop moves left past any element where the value at nums right is greater than the value at nums left times k, converting both sides to long long to avoid overflow, given that nums i can be as large as ten to the ninth.
4:cpp:3: After each adjustment, best is set to the larger of the current best value and the window size computed as right minus left minus one, so it records the largest window size seen so far using this particular pointer offset.
4:cpp:4: Because the left pointer moves monotonically across the entire scan, the two-pointer portion runs in linear time after the linearithmic time sorting step.
4:cpp:5: The function returns n minus best, which converts the size of the largest window that can be kept into the minimum number of elements that must be removed from the array.
5:javascript:1: The exist function iterates over every cell in the board, row by row and column by column, using nested loops, and it calls the dfs closure for each one because a matching path could start anywhere.
5:javascript:2: Inside the depth first search, the base case where the index equals the word length returns true as soon as every character has been matched in order.
5:javascript:3: The guard clause checks for out of bounds coordinates or a character mismatch at the board cell at row r and column c compared to the word at position i, and it returns false immediately when either happens, which prunes invalid branches early.
5:javascript:4: Before recursing further, the cell is temporarily set to the hash symbol to mark it as visited for the current path, and all four directions—down, up, right, and left—are tried using logical OR so the search stops at the first direction that succeeds; the original character is then restored by setting the board cell back to the saved temporary value so other starting points can reuse that cell.
5:javascript:5: This mark then restore technique is what allows the same two dimensional array to be reused for backtracking without needing a separate visited set.
5:python:1: The outer loop method goes through every cell, meaning each row and column pair in the grid, and starts a depth first search call with those coordinates and a starting index of zero, because the word could begin anywhere on the board.
5:python:2: Inside the depth first search function, the base case where i equals the length of the word returns true right away, meaning every character has already been matched successfully.
5:python:3: The bounds and mismatch check, meaning that r is less than zero or at least the number of rows, or c is less than zero or at least the number of columns, or the board cell at row r and column c is not equal to the i th character of the word, returns false for any invalid or non matching path, pruning the search early.
5:python:4: Before recursing, the current cell is temporarily overwritten with a hash character so it cannot be revisited within the same path, and the search explores all four directions, down, up, right, and left, using a logical or so it stops as soon as one direction succeeds; afterward, the original character is restored by assigning the saved temporary value back to the cell regardless of the outcome, which is the key backtracking step.
5:python:5: This marking-and-restoring pattern allows the same board to be reused for every starting cell, while ensuring that no cell is used more than once within a single candidate path.
5:java:1: The exist method stores the board, the word, the number of rows, and the number of columns as instance fields, so the recursive depth first search helper does not need to pass them as parameters on every call, and then it loops over every cell as a possible starting point.
5:java:2: The base case, when the index i reaches the length of the word, returns true once every character of the target word has been matched along the current path.
5:java:3: The guard condition checks for out of bounds indices and a character mismatch in a single line, and returns false immediately to prune that branch.
5:java:4: Before recursing, the current cell is overwritten with a hash symbol to mark it as visited, and the method explores all four neighboring cells by using boolean OR so it stops early as soon as any direction succeeds; the original character is restored right away by assigning the saved value back to the cell, immediately after the recursive call finishes, no matter whether the recursion succeeded or not.
5:java:5: This temporary marking backtracking approach keeps the algorithm using only extra space proportional to the length of the word, beyond the input board itself.
5:cpp:1: The public exist method loops over every cell on the board and calls the private dfs helper from each cell, because the matching word could start in any position.
5:cpp:2: Inside the depth-first search, the base case where i is equal to the word length, after converting i to an integer, returns true once the entire word has been matched one character at a time.
5:cpp:3: The bounds and mismatch check returns false right away for coordinates that are out of range or for a character that does not match, which prevents the search from wasting time on invalid paths.
5:cpp:4: Before recursing, the current cell is overwritten with a hash symbol to mark it as visited, and the four neighboring directions are explored using boolean OR so the recursion stops as soon as a success is found; afterward, the original character is put back into the cell, so the board is left unmodified for the next starting cell.
5:cpp:5: Passing the board by reference and modifying it directly avoids the overhead of creating a separate visited matrix, while still correctly undoing changes after each explored path.
6:javascript:1: The function maintains left and right pointers that span the array, computes mid as the whole number result of dividing left plus right by two inside a while loop that continues while left is less than or equal to right, and returns mid immediately if the element at nums at mid equals the target.
6:javascript:2: The comparison of nums at the left index with nums at the middle index tells whether the left part of the current window is the sorted half of the rotated array.
6:javascript:3: After it is sorted, the code checks whether the target lies between nums at the left index and nums at the middle index, but not including the middle value; if it does, it sets right to mid minus one, otherwise it sets left to mid plus one.
6:javascript:4: When the right portion is sorted instead, the symmetric test that says the target is greater than the middle value and the target is less than or equal to the right value determines which side to discard.
6:javascript:5: Because in any rotated array, exactly one half around any middle position is guaranteed to be sorted, this method still removes half the search space in each iteration, so the algorithm remains on the order of log n.
6:python:1: The algorithm keeps two pointers, left and right, that cover the entire array, and each time through the while loop it calculates mid as the integer half of left plus right.
6:python:2: If the value at nums in the middle position equals the target, the index is returned immediately.
6:python:3: Otherwise, the code checks whether the value at nums at left is less than or equal to the value at nums at mid to determine whether the left half of the current window is the sorted portion; if it is, and the target is between nums at left and nums at mid, then the search continues in the left half by setting right to mid minus one, otherwise it moves to the right half with left set to mid plus one.
6:python:4: If instead the right half is the sorted portion, the same logic applies in reverse: if the target is between nums mid and nums right, including nums right, then the search continues to the right, otherwise it moves to the left.
6:python:5: This half-sorted check is the key insight that lets binary search still work in logarithmic time on a rotated array, because exactly one of the two halves around the middle is guaranteed to be in ascending order.
6:java:1: Two pointers, left and right, bound the search window, and mid is recomputed each iteration of the while loop as the average of left and right, until nums at mid equals the target and is returned.
6:java:2: The condition that the value at nums left is less than or equal to the value at nums mid identifies whether the left half of the window is the sorted segment of the rotated array.
6:java:3: When the left half is sorted, the code checks whether the target lies between the value at nums at the left index and the value at nums at the middle index, not including the middle value; if it does, it searches the left side by setting right to one less than mid, otherwise it searches the right side by setting left to one more than mid.
6:java:4: When the right half is sorted instead, the mirrored check that the target is greater than the middle element and less than or equal to the rightmost element determines whether to move left or right.
6:java:5: Because one half of any rotated sorted array around a pivot midpoint is always correctly ordered, this decision process keeps binary search’s logarithmic time complexity, in other words proportional to log of the array length, even though the array as a whole is not fully sorted.
6:cpp:1: The search method keeps left and right indices, then computes the midpoint as left plus the difference between right and left divided by two inside a while loop that continues as long as left is less than or equal to right. It uses this overflow safe midpoint formula instead of averaging left and right directly, and it returns the midpoint immediately as soon as the value at nums at that midpoint equals the target.
6:cpp:2: The condition that nums at left is less than or equal to nums at mid determines whether the left half of the current window is the correctly sorted segment.
6:cpp:3: If it is, the code checks whether the target lies within the range from nums at left up to nums at mid, but not including nums at mid, to decide whether to search the left half, setting right to mid minus one, or to search the right half, setting left to mid plus one.
6:cpp:4: If the right half is sorted instead, the mirrored condition, target is greater than the middle value and target is less than or equal to the rightmost value, makes the equivalent decision for that side.
6:cpp:5: Because a rotated sorted array always has at least one correctly ordered half around any midpoint, this technique preserves binary search’s logarithmic time complexity while correctly accounting for the rotation.
7:javascript:1: A plain array named queue tracks the asteroids that have survived collisions so far, and the input is processed in one left to right pass.
7:javascript:2: For each asteroid, the alive flag is set to true at the start, and a while loop keeps running as long as the asteroid is still marked alive, is moving left, and the queue has a positive value at the first position.
7:javascript:3: Inside the loop, the variable front is read from the first element of the queue; if front is smaller in magnitude than the incoming asteroid, then front is removed from the queue using shift and the loop continues; if front exactly matches in magnitude, then both asteroids are destroyed by removing front with shift and setting alive to false; otherwise, front survives, so alive is set to false without changing the queue.
7:javascript:4: Once the while loop finishes, if the variable alive is still true, the asteroid named a is added to the end of the queue.
7:javascript:5: After the complete scan, the queue array is returned directly as the final list of asteroids that survive.
7:python:1: The algorithm keeps a deque called queue that represents the asteroids that have survived collisions so far, and it processes the input array from left to right.
7:python:2: For each new asteroid, a, the alive flag is set to true, and an inner while loop runs as long as the asteroid is still alive, it is moving left, and there is a positive asteroid at the front of the queue that it could collide with.
7:python:3: Inside the loop, if the front of the queue has a smaller magnitude than the incoming asteroid, the front is removed using popleft and the loop continues to check the next one; if the front has exactly the same magnitude, both asteroids are destroyed by removing the front and setting alive to False; otherwise the front has a larger magnitude and survives, so alive is set to False without changing the queue.
7:python:4: After the while loop finishes, if alive is still true, asteroid a is added to the back of the queue as a survivor.
7:python:5: After every asteroid has been processed, the function returns list of the remaining asteroids in the queue, in their original left to right order.
7:java:1: A Queue of integers named queue holds the asteroids that have survived collisions processed so far, and the array is scanned once from left to right.
7:java:2: For each asteroid, an “alive” flag is initially set to true, and a while loop keeps running as long as the asteroid is alive, it is moving left, and the queue is not empty and the value at the front of the queue is positive.
7:java:3: Inside the loop, the front value is read from the queue using peek. If the front’s magnitude is smaller than the incoming asteroid’s magnitude, the front is removed from the queue using poll, and the loop continues. If the front’s magnitude exactly matches the incoming asteroid’s magnitude, both asteroids are destroyed by polling the front and setting alive to false. Otherwise, the front survives, so alive is simply set to false.
7:java:4: After the loop, if the variable alive is still true, the current asteroid is added to the queue using queue dot add with a.
7:java:5: Once all asteroids are processed, the contents of the queue are copied into an integer array called result in the order they are iterated, and that array is returned as the final surviving asteroid field.
7:cpp:1: A double ended queue of integers named queue stores the asteroids that have survived collisions processed so far, and the input vector is scanned once from left to right.
7:cpp:2: For each asteroid, the boolean alive flag starts as true, and a while loop keeps running as long as the asteroid is still alive, it is moving left, and the deque is not empty with a positive value at the front.
7:cpp:3: Inside the loop, if the value at the front of the queue is smaller in magnitude than the incoming asteroid, it is removed using pop front, and the loop continues; if it exactly matches in magnitude, both asteroids are destroyed by popping the front and setting alive to false; otherwise, the front asteroid survives, so alive is simply set to false.
7:cpp:4: After the loop, if alive is still true, the asteroid a is added to the back of the deque using push back of a.
7:cpp:5: Once every asteroid has been processed, the deque is converted into a vector of integers using its begin and end iterators, and then returned as the final result.
8:javascript:1: The LRU cache is built around a native map object, and the JavaScript specification guarantees that it will iterate keys in insertion order, which makes it useful for tracking how recently items were used.
8:javascript:2: The get method returns minus one if the key is missing; otherwise it reads the value, deletes the key, and inserts it again using cache dot set, which moves it to the end of the Map's iteration order to mark it as the most recently used.
8:javascript:3: The put method works the same way by deleting the key first if it already exists, so the later set moves it to the end instead of leaving it in its old position, and then it assigns the new value.
8:javascript:4: After inserting, if this cache size exceeds capacity, the code retrieves the first key using this cache keys next value, which is the least recently used entry because maps preserve insertion order, and then it deletes that entry.
8:javascript:5: This delete-then-reinsert pattern is what lets a plain map act like least-recently-used ordering without using a separate doubly linked list.
8:python:1: The LRU cache class wraps an ordered dictionary, meaning a dictionary that also keeps track of insertion order and supports efficient reordering.
8:python:2: When you call get, if the key is missing it returns negative one; otherwise it calls self dot cache dot move to end, to mark that key as the most recently used before returning its value, since an OrderedDict keeps recently accessed keys at the end.
8:python:3: On a put operation, if the key already exists, it is first moved to the end to refresh its recency, and then the cache assignment inserts or updates the entry.
8:python:4: After inserting an item, if the length of the cache is greater than its capacity, calling popitem with last set to false removes the entry at the front of the ordered dictionary, which is always the least recently used entry.
8:python:5: Because an OrderedDict supports constant time move to the end and constant time pop from the front, both get and put run in constant time, as required by the problem’s constraints.
8:java:1: The LRUCache class extends LinkedHashMap of Integer keys to Integer values, and in its constructor it calls super with the capacity, a load factor of zero point seven five, and true, where that final true argument enables access order iteration instead of the default insertion order.
8:java:2: This means every time you get or put something into the underlying map, it automatically moves that entry to the end of the internal iteration order, marking it as the most recently used without any manual tracking.
8:java:3: The get method simply calls getOrDefault with the key and a default value of negative one, relying on LinkedHashMap’s access order behavior to update recency as a side effect of the lookup.
8:java:4: The put method calls the parent’s put directly, and the overridden removeEldestEntry map entry method is automatically invoked by LinkedHashMap after each insertion; returning that the current size is greater than the capacity from this override tells the map to evict the eldest, meaning the least recently used, entry whenever the cache exceeds its capacity.
8:java:5: This design relies entirely on the built-in behavior of LinkedHashMap, so there is no need for any extra list or pointer manipulation.
8:cpp:1: The LRU cache class maintains two data structures together: a list of key value pairs called order that stores key value pairs from most recently used to least recently used, and an unordered map called map that provides constant time access to the position of any node in that list.
8:cpp:2: On a get, if the key is not found it returns minus one; otherwise it uses order dot splice, with order begin and the range starting at the node identified by it dot second, to move that node to the front of the list in constant time without reallocating, and then it returns its value.
8:cpp:3: When putting a key, if it already exists, its value is updated in place, and the same splice operation moves it to the front to mark it as freshly used.
8:cpp:4: If the key is new, inserting it uses order emplace front with the key and value to put it at the front, and map at that key is set to the iterator to the front of the list; if the map then exceeds capacity, the back of the list, which is the least recently used entry, is read, erased from the map, and removed from the back of the list.
8:cpp:5: Because list iterators stay valid after splice operations, unlike vector iterators, this combination of a linked list and a hash map achieves true constant time, for both retrieval and insertion.
9:javascript:1: The function starts by splitting the input string, s, into an array of single characters using s dot split with an empty string as the separator, because JavaScript strings are immutable, so each character has to be reassigned individually.
9:javascript:2: A for loop runs starting at the length of the string minus one and continues down to zero, walking backward through the string from its last character to its first.
9:javascript:3: For each index, shifted is computed by taking the character code at that index minus the code for a, adding the corresponding shift value, and then taking the result modulo twenty-six, which converts the character into its zero-based position in the alphabet using its character code, applies the shift from shifts i, and wraps with modulo twenty-six so the shift cycles correctly past z.
9:javascript:4: String dot from char code, taking the value of ninety seven plus shifted, converts the numeric result back into a character and stores it in result at position i, using the loop index directly to pair each character with its shift entry.
9:javascript:5: Once the loop completes, result dot join with an empty string reassembles the character array into the final shifted string, and then it is returned as the answer.
9:python:1: The function first copies the input string into a mutable list called result, because Python strings can’t be changed in place and individual characters must be reassigned.
9:python:2: A for loop iterates i starting at the length of the string minus one and continues down to zero, processing the string from its last character back to its first.
9:python:3: For each index, shifted is computed by taking the letter’s alphabet position starting from zero, subtracting the alphabet position of the letter a, adding the corresponding shift value from shifts at that index, and then taking the result modulo twenty-six so that z cycles back to a.
9:python:4: The computed value is converted back into a character by taking the character code for a, adding the shifted amount, and then turning that code into a character, and it is written into result at position i, using i directly to look up the corresponding entry in the shifts array.
9:python:5: Once the loop finishes, the phrase join result reassembles the list of characters into the final shifted string, and that string is returned as the answer.
9:java:1: The method begins by converting the input string into a character array called result using s to char array, because Java strings are immutable and each character must be updated in place.
9:java:2: A for loop starts at one less than the string length and counts down to zero, processing the string from its last character back toward the first.
9:java:3: For each index, shifted is computed by taking result at that index minus the letter a, adding shifts at that index, and then taking the remainder when divided by twenty-six; this maps the character to its zero-based position in the alphabet, adds the shifts value for that index, and wraps around with twenty-six so that values past z cycle back to a.
9:java:4: The result is converted back into a character by adding the shifted value to the character 'a', then casting that sum to a char, and the converted character is stored back into result at the position i, using the loop index i directly to match each character with its corresponding entry in the shifts array.
9:java:5: After the loop finishes, creating a new String from result turns the character array back into a String, and that String is returned as the final answer.
9:cpp:1: The function starts by copying the input string s into a modifiable string named result, because the characters must be changed as the algorithm continues.
9:cpp:2: A for loop starts at the integer value of the string length minus one and counts down to zero, moving backward through the string from its last character to its first.
9:cpp:3: For each index, shifted is computed by taking the difference between result at that index and the letter a, adding the corresponding shifts value, and then taking the remainder after dividing by twenty-six, which maps the character to its zero based position in the alphabet, applies the shift, and wraps correctly from z back to a by cycling with modulo twenty-six.
9:cpp:4: The computed offset is added directly to a, and stored back into result at position i, using the loop index i to match each character with its shifts entry.
9:cpp:5: Once the loop is finished, the modified result string is returned directly as the final shifted string.
10:javascript:1: The searchRange function defines a lowerBound closure that performs a binary search using lo and hi pointers, converging on the first index whose value is not less than val by increasing lo whenever nums at mid is less than val and reducing hi otherwise.
10:javascript:2: The variable left is set by calling lower bound with the target, and if left equals the length of the nums array or if nums at index left does not equal the target, the function immediately returns minus one, minus one because the target is not present.
10:javascript:3: Otherwise, the right side is computed by calling lower bound on the target a second time and then subtracting one, reusing the same helper to anchor the end of the target’s range.
10:javascript:4: Using Math floor of the quantity of lo plus hi divided by two, or using the bitwise right shift of lo plus hi by one position, for the midpoint keeps each binary search step running in logarithmic time with respect to n.
10:javascript:5: Both the left result and the right result are returned together as an array with two elements, producing the final answer after only two calls to lower bound.
10:python:1: The searchRange method calls bisect dot bisect left with nums and target, which performs a binary search to find the leftmost index where the target could be inserted while keeping nums sorted, and it stores this index as left.
10:python:2: If the left pointer reaches the length of the list, or if the value at that index is not the target, the function short-circuits and returns negative one comma negative one, because the target is not present in the array.
10:python:3: Otherwise, the right boundary is computed by calling bisect left again on nums with the target and then subtracting one, using the same lower-bound index as the reference point for the end of the target’s range.
10:python:4: Then the left and right results are combined into one list and returned as the final start and end answer.
10:python:5: Because bisect left runs in logarithmic time and it is called two times, the overall solution stays within logarithmic time complexity as required by the problem’s constraints.
10:java:1: The searchRange method first calls the private lowerBound of nums and target, which performs a binary search using two pointers named lo and hi that move closer together until they identify the first index whose value is not less than the target; this index becomes left.
10:java:2: If left equals the length of nums, or if the value at nums at index left is not equal to the target, the method immediately returns a two element array containing negative one and negative one, because the target does not appear in the array.
10:java:3: Otherwise, the right endpoint is computed by calling lowerBound on the same list with the same target a second time, then subtracting one, reusing the same binary-search logic to anchor the end of the range.
10:java:4: The lowerBound helper narrows lo and hi by setting mid to half of lo plus hi using a right shift, moves lo up when nums at mid is less than val, and otherwise shrinks hi, until the two values converge.
10:java:5: Then both the left and right values are returned together as a two element array of integers, giving the answer in logarithmic time, since the lower bound function is called only twice.
10:cpp:1: The searchRange method calls lower_bound, passing the start and end of the sorted vector and the target value, which performs a binary search to find the first position whose value is not less than the target; then subtracting the beginning iterator turns that position into an integer index that is stored as left.
10:cpp:2: If left equals the size of nums or the value at nums at index left is not equal to target, the method immediately returns negative one, negative one, because the target is not present in the array.
10:cpp:3: Otherwise, the right boundary is computed by calling lower bound on the array a second time with the target value, then converting the resulting iterator into an index and subtracting one.
10:cpp:4: Because lower_bound is a standard library binary search, each call runs in logarithmic time over the sorted vector.
10:cpp:5: The left and right values are returned together as a two element vector, producing the final start and end result after two lower bound searches that each take time proportional to log n.
